title: Polio case and death rates in the United States
subtitle: The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both [wild-](#dod:wild-poliovirus) and [vaccine-derived poliovirus](#dod:vaccine-derived-poliovirus) infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.
caption:
Polio case and death rates in the United States
The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both wild- and vaccine-derived poliovirus infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.

Across the United States from 1910 to 2023, the reported rates of polio cases and deaths per 100,000 people show a transition from fluctuating endemic levels to a sustained period of near-zero incidence. The visualization plots two distinct time-series lines: an orange line representing the polio case rate and a blue line representing the polio death rate. The vertical axis measures the rate per 100,000 population, ranging from 0 to 40. Between 1910 and the early 1940s, the case rate oscillates frequently, with visible peaks reaching approximately 13 cases per 100,000 around 1916 and 1930. The death rate follows a similar but lower trajectory, generally remaining below 2 deaths per 100,000 during this early period. A sharp escalation occurs in the 1940s and 1950s, where the case rate climbs steeply to reach its highest visible point of approximately 36 cases per 100,000 around 1952. Following this peak, the orange line drops precipitously, falling below 5 cases per 100,000 by 1960 and reaching zero shortly thereafter. The blue death rate line mirrors this decline, dropping from its local maximums in the 1950s to a flat line at zero for the remainder of the timeline. The data includes both wild- and vaccine-derived poliovirus infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases. From 1965 through 2023, both lines remain flat along the horizontal axis at the zero mark, showing no visible variation in the reported rates over these decades.
